questions.question: Why does the two-tower model enable faster serving than scoring every user-item pair at request time?
options: The user embedding is computed once per request, then ANN search retrieves top-K candidates from the pre-indexed item embedding space in milliseconds — avoiding O(users × items) scoring.
The two-tower model runs on the client device, eliminating server round-trips.
The two-tower model requires fewer features than matrix factorization.
ANN search is only needed for cold start users, not for subscribers with history.
questions.explanation: Precomputing item embeddings offline and building an ANN index allows retrieval in single-digit milliseconds at request time. Full cross-product scoring at 270M users × 36,000 titles would be orders of magnitude too slow.

questions.question: What is the core weakness of pure collaborative filtering for a new title?
options: A new title has no interaction history, so there are no co-watch signals to learn from — it will not appear in collaborative filtering results until enough subscribers have watched it.
Collaborative filtering requires a GPU cluster that is not available for new title ingestion.
New titles must be encoded in a specific codec before collaborative filtering can index them.
Collaborative filtering only works for returning subscribers, not new ones.
questions.explanation: This is the item cold start problem. Content-based filtering and editorial promotion are standard mitigations until a new title accumulates enough interaction data.

questions.question: A subscriber's long-term genre affinity feature is updated daily, but their short-term watch history is updated every few minutes. Why is this split justified?
options: Long-term preferences change slowly and are expensive to recompute; short-term signals change rapidly and directly impact the current session's relevance.
Long-term features are more accurate and should not be updated too frequently.
Short-term features are only used for cold start subscribers.
Daily updates for all features would overload the feature store with write traffic.
questions.explanation: A subscriber who just watched a horror movie should see related suggestions immediately. Their overall genre affinity changes over months. Different freshness requirements justify different update cadences.

questions.question: What is the correct fallback when the recommendation API latency spikes above the SLA?
options: Serve pre-computed recommendations from EVCache (Memcached) — they may be slightly stale but are always available at sub-millisecond latency.
Return an empty homepage so subscribers know the system is degraded.
Redirect subscribers to the search page until the API recovers.
Compute recommendations synchronously using a simpler model to meet the latency budget.
questions.explanation: Pre-computed recommendations in EVCache are the designed fallback path. Stale recommendations are far better than no recommendations or a blank homepage.

questions.question: How does homepage row ranking differ from within-row title ranking?
options: Row ranking decides which row types to show this subscriber and in what order; within-row ranking decides which titles to surface within each chosen row type.
Row ranking uses a larger neural network than within-row ranking.
Row ranking only applies to the cold start path; returning subscribers see fixed row types.
Within-row ranking is deterministic (alphabetical by title); only row order is personalized.
questions.explanation: These are two separate personalization decisions stacked on top of each other. A subscriber might see 20 rows out of 50 possible row types, then personalized titles within each.

questions.question: What is the risk of rebuilding the ANN index during a model redeployment?
options: There is a window where the new model weights are serving but the ANN index reflects the old embeddings, causing score mismatch and potentially poor recommendations.
ANN index rebuilds always take longer than 24 hours, making daily retraining impractical.
The ANN index must be rebuilt on every feature store update, not just on model updates.
ANN index rebuilds require all subscribers to log out and log back in.
questions.explanation: The mitigation is to keep the previous model's index hot and switch atomically — or to warm the new index before cutting traffic to it.

questions.question: Which metric best captures whether a recommendation improvement actually made subscribers happier?
options: Long-term retention rate and completion rate per recommended title — whether subscribers actually watched and finished content the system recommended.
Homepage recommendation API latency — lower latency means better user experience.
Click-through rate alone, measured over a 1-hour window.
The number of recommendations that were shown, not clicked.
questions.explanation: Click-through rate captures engagement but not satisfaction. Completion rate and long-term retention are more reliable proxies for whether the recommendation was genuinely useful.

questions.question: What is the most important cold start mitigation for a new subscriber's first session?
options: Accelerate short-term signal updates so that after 3-5 plays, recommendations already reflect early preferences — bridging the gap until enough history accumulates for full personalization.
Show only the top-50 globally trending titles until the subscriber has watched 100 titles.
Disable recommendations entirely for the first 30 days and rely on search.
Ask subscribers to rate 50 titles before the homepage loads.
questions.explanation: The first session is the most critical for retention. Fast feedback loops on early signals — even from a single watched title — dramatically improve the first-session experience.
